text: The suggestions so far have assumed a one-time transfer. If you want to share files on a regular basis, Syncthing (<https://syncthing.net/>) is a good choice. I use it for sharing data across devices (Linux, macOS, Android) and with family members. Its unit of data is a shared directory, which is identified by a UUID. Anyone who has the UUID can access the shared data (by default, you can configure more restrictive rules). Data exchange is P2P, but machines find each other (again, by default) via a public relay. With the standard configuration, you create a directory, add it to Syncthing, and then send the UUID to the people who you want to share with.

Anyone who has the UUID can access the shared data (by default, you can configure more restrictive rules). Data exchange is P2P, but machines find each other (again, by default) via a public relay. With the standard configuration, you create a directory, add it to Syncthing, and then send the UUID to the people who you want to share with.

text:
The Development of the C Language by Dennis M. Ritchie

The C programming language was devised in the early 1970s as a system implementation language for the nascent Unix operating system. Derived from the typeless language BCPL, it evolved a type structure; created on a tiny machine as a tool to improve a meager programming environment, it has become one of the dominant languages of today. This paper studies its evolution.

<http://cm.bell-labs.co/who/dmr/chist.html>

text: What thinkers or groups not related to mathematics or computer science most influence your thinking about programming and computers?

elements.text: What thinkers or groups not related to mathematics or computer science most influence your thinking about programming and computers?

text:
Foucault’s thinking on power and politics manifest in a lot of how I approach designing systems, and programming. 

Different, I also find that descriptions of surface area, and edges from permaculture design show up in my head a lot. 

I always imagine functions in pipelines like characters in a book by Djuna Barnes or Virginia Woolf.

elements.text: Hopefully this isn't too ranty for this channel:

attachments.text:
I like to say that software is often just bureaucracy by other means (though it could be oh so much more).

This might be the single-most powerful example of that on the internet: <https://rclone.org/drive/#making-your-own-client-id>

The reason I sought this page out:
- I want to download a copy of my baby photos in case Google shuts me out in future.

- Google Takeout is basically unusable if you have more data than you can download in 30 minutes. (<https://lethallava.land/notes/9if8o9ngl13900d9>)

- So I saved my Takeout files to Google Drive (not yet taken out! <https://merveilles.town/@akkartik/110892364898712223>)

- Then I had to find a way to get files out of Google Drive. Which leads to the above page.

Google should be required to report reading burdens like the OMB does for US govt. forms.
attachments.title: Kartik Agaram (@akkartik)

text:
I recently heard someone say “if the state has a monopoly on violence, than schools have a monopoly on discipline.”

Ever since I heard it, I’ve been trying to articulate this quip to be about the tech industry — and, while I haven’t succeeded at that quip-creation, yet…this feels all connected.

text:
related: a useful description of authoritarianism is when power is so exclusively centralized that the public is powerless to effect change.

in a lot of ways, google has creeped up on becoming an authoritarian power over this sorta data
